revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Oguni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = Honden and Maidono of Oguni Jinja | type = | dedication = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi District, Shizuoka | phone = 0538-89-7302 | website = http://www.okunijinja.jp/ }} The {{nihongo|'''Oguni Shrine'''|小國神社|Oguni Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]] in [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka|Shūchi District]] [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is one of two shrines claiming to hold the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a National Intangible Cultural Property. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Mishima Taisha is the {{nihongo|Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto|大己貴命}}. There are numerous small subsidiary shrines to various ''kami'' within the shrine grounds. ==History== The date of Oguni Shrine’s foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]] as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine’s grounds. The shrine appears in historical records in an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records. It has been styles as the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. [[Takeda Shingen]] worshipped at the shrine in 1572, and [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1575. Ieyasu later contributed to the rebuilding of the ''[[Honden]]'', and subsequent generations of Tokugawa [[Shogun]] continued to support the shrine. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was proclaimed a prefectural shrine (県社) in 1873, and was granted the rank of [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|''Kokuhei Shōsha'']] under [[State Shinto]] in 1875. Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. (1996). ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. London: [[RoutledgeCurzon]]. 10-ISBN 1-873-41063-8 * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[ja:小國神社]]

This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a National Intangible Cultural Property. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-struct-stub}} {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = [[Honden]] and [[Maidono]] of Okuni Jinja | type = | dedication = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi District, Shizuoka | phone = 0538-89-7302 | website = http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, also known as '''Okuni jinja''', is a [[Japan]]ese [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], in [[Shizuoka Prefecture]].Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Okuni Jinja is the {{nihongo|Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto|大己貴命}}. There are numerous small subsidiary shrines to various ''kami'' within the shrine grounds. ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine’s foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]] as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine’s grounds. The shrine appears in historical records in an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records. It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. [[Takeda Shingen]] worshipped at the shrine in 1572, and [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] in 1575. Ieyasu later contributed to the rebuilding of the ''[[Honden]]'', and subsequent generations of Tokugawa [[Shogun]] continued to support the shrine. Okuni is one of two shrines claiming to hold the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya," p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], it was proclaimed a prefectural shrine (県社) in 1873. In the system of ranked Shinto shrines, Okuni was listed in 1875 among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''Kokuhei Shōsha''|国幣小社}}.[[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a National Intangible Cultural Property. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-struct-stub}} {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine. {{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|Natuonal shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|Kokuhei Shōsha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine. {{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|Natuonal shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|Kokuhei Shōsha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}}

(1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine. {{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|Natuonal shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|Kokuhei Shōsha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine. {{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|Natuonal shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|Kokuhei Shōsha}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei Kosha]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}}

It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine. {{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|Natuonal shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine. {{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|Natuonal shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}}

(1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]][[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}}

The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia]][[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia]][[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia]][[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:pages from wikipedia]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}}

It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia7]] [[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia7]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia7]] [[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia7]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia7]] [[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia7]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia21]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}}

Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia07]] [[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia07]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia07]] [[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia07]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia07]] [[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia07]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia29]] [[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia29]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia21]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia07]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia07]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia07]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia07]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia07]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia07]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia29]] [[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia29]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia21]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Oguni shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}}
