小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), 小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), {{Ichinomiya}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = Ogunijinja01.jpg | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|51|N|137|53|57|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi|Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 14 | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[shōgun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd rank |国幣小社|[[kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard.
