The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank |国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[:en:Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station|Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[:en:Tenryū Hamanako Railroad|Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[:en:List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] }} [[en:Okuni Shrine]] [[en:Oguni shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Okuni-jinja (Mori)}} [[fr:Okuni-jinja]] [[ja:小国神社]] [[zh:小國神社]] {{wikidata link|Q421597}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

小國神社 | image = File:Okunijinja-Haiden, Mori-machi Shuchi-gun Shizuoka Japan 2013.06.29.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[:en:Maidono|Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.8475|N|137.89916667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style ={{ill|Taisha-zukuri|WD=Q387874}} | festival = April 14 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Mori|WD=Q1345571}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the town of [[:en:Mori, Shizuoka|Mori]], [[:en:Shūchi District, Shizuoka| Shūchi District]], [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Tōtōmi Province|Tōtōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank |国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[:en:Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station|Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[:en:Tenryū Hamanako Railroad|Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[:en:List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| }} [[en:Okuni Shrine]] [[en:Oguni shrine]] {{Commons category|Okuni-jinja (Mori)}} [[fr:Okuni-jinja]] [[ja:小国神社]] [[zh:小國神社]] {{wikidata link|Q421597}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] era of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank |国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from [[:en:Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station|Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station]] on the [[:en:Tenryū Hamanako Railroad|Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[:en:List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Okuni Shrine]] [[en:Oguni shrine]] {{Commons category|Okuni-jinja (Mori)}} [[fr:Okuni-jinja]] [[ja:小国神社]] [[zh:小國神社]] {{wikidata link|Q421597}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from {{ill|Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station|en|Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station|WD=Q5370068|lt=Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station}} on the [[:en:Tenryū Hamanako Railroad|Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * {{ill|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|en|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|WD=Q111360896|lt=List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties}} * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Okuni Shrine]] [[en:Oguni shrine]] {{Commons category|Okuni-jinja (Mori)}} [[fr:Okuni-jinja]] [[ja:小国神社]] [[zh:小國神社]] {{wikidata link|Q421597}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[co:commons:Category:Okuni-jinja (Mori)]][[en:Okuni Shrine]][[fr:Okuni-jinja]][[ja:小国神社]][[zh:小國神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Okuni-jinja (Mori)]][[en:Okuni Shrine]][[fr:Okuni-jinja]][[ja:小国神社]][[zh:小國神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Okuni-jinja (Mori)]][[en:Okuni Shrine]][[fr:Okuni-jinja]][[ja:小国神社]][[zh:小國神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q421597]] Wikidata name: Oguni Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = File:Okunijinja-Haiden, Mori-machi Shuchi-gun Shizuoka Japan 2013.06.29.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[:en:Maidono|Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.8475|N|137.89916667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style ={{ill|Taisha-zukuri|WD=Q387874}} | festival = April 14 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Mori|WD=Q1345571}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the town of {{ill|Mori, Shizuoka|en|Mori, Shizuoka|WD=Q1345571|lt=Mori}}, {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|en|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|WD=Q1205501|lt= Shūchi District}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|en|Tōtōmi Province|WD=Q1048864|lt=Tōtōmi Province}}.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei}}) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen}} invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} era of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}, the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank |国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from {{ill|Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station|en|Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station|WD=Q5370068|lt=Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station}} on the [[:en:Tenryū Hamanako Railroad|Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * {{ill|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|en|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|WD=Q111360896|lt=List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties}} * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category}} *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Okuni Shrine]] [[en:Oguni shrine]] {{Commons category|Okuni-jinja (Mori)}} [[fr:Okuni-jinja]] [[ja:小国神社]] [[zh:小國神社]] {{wikidata link|Q421597}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

(1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. This performance is said to date to the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * {{ill|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|en|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|WD=Q111360896|lt=List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties}} * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:Category]] *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Okuni Shrine]] [[en:Oguni shrine]] {{Commons category|Okuni-jinja (Mori)}} [[fr:Okuni-jinja]] [[ja:小国神社]] [[zh:小國神社]] {{wikidata link|Q421597}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[co:commons:Category:Okuni-jinja (Mori)]][[en:Okuni Shrine]][[fr:Okuni-jinja]][[ja:小国神社]][[zh:小國神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Okuni-jinja (Mori)]][[en:Okuni Shrine]][[fr:Okuni-jinja]][[ja:小国神社]][[zh:小國神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Okuni-jinja (Mori)]][[en:Okuni Shrine]][[fr:Okuni-jinja]][[ja:小国神社]][[zh:小國神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q421597]] Wikidata name: Oguni Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = File:Okunijinja-Haiden, Mori-machi Shuchi-gun Shizuoka Japan 2013.06.29.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[:en:Maidono|Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.8475|N|137.89916667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style ={{ill|Taisha-zukuri|WD=Q387874}} | festival = April 14 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Mori|WD=Q1345571}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the town of {{ill|Mori, Shizuoka|en|Mori, Shizuoka|WD=Q1345571|lt=Mori}}, {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|en|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|WD=Q1205501|lt= Shūchi District}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|en|Tōtōmi Province|WD=Q1048864|lt=Tōtōmi Province}}.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei}}) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen}} invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} era of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}, the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank |国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from {{ill|Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station|en|Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station|WD=Q5370068|lt=Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station}} on the [[:en:Tenryū Hamanako Railroad|Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * {{ill|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|en|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|WD=Q111360896|lt=List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties}} * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:Category]] *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Okuni Shrine]] [[en:Oguni shrine]] {{Commons category|Okuni-jinja (Mori)}} [[fr:Okuni-jinja]] [[ja:小国神社]] [[zh:小國神社]] {{wikidata link|Q421597}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

This performance is said to date to the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * {{ill|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|en|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|WD=Q111360896|lt=List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties}} * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:Category]] *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Okuni Shrine]] [[en:Oguni shrine]] {{Commons category|Okuni-jinja (Mori)}} [[fr:Okuni-jinja]] [[ja:小国神社]] [[zh:小國神社]] {{wikidata link|Q421597}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]]

[[co:commons:Category:Okuni-jinja (Mori)]][[en:Okuni Shrine]][[fr:Okuni-jinja]][[ja:小国神社]][[zh:小國神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Okuni-jinja (Mori)]][[en:Okuni Shrine]][[fr:Okuni-jinja]][[ja:小国神社]][[zh:小國神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Okuni-jinja (Mori)]][[en:Okuni Shrine]][[fr:Okuni-jinja]][[ja:小国神社]][[zh:小國神社]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = File:Okunijinja-Haiden, Mori-machi Shuchi-gun Shizuoka Japan 2013.06.29.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[:en:Maidono|Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.8475|N|137.89916667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style ={{ill|Taisha-zukuri|WD=Q387874}} | festival = April 14 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Mori|WD=Q1345571}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the town of {{ill|Mori, Shizuoka|en|Mori, Shizuoka|WD=Q1345571|lt=Mori}}, {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|en|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|WD=Q1205501|lt= Shūchi District}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|en|Tōtōmi Province|WD=Q1048864|lt=Tōtōmi Province}}.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei}}) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen}} invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} era of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}, the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank |国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from {{ill|Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station|en|Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station|WD=Q5370068|lt=Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station}} on the [[:en:Tenryū Hamanako Railroad|Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * {{ill|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|en|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|WD=Q111360896|lt=List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties}} * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:Category]] *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Okuni Shrine]] [[en:Oguni shrine]] {{Commons category|Okuni-jinja (Mori)}} [[fr:Okuni-jinja]] [[ja:小国神社]] [[zh:小國神社]] {{wikidata link|Q421597}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]]

[[co:commons:Category:Okuni-jinja (Mori)]][[en:Okuni Shrine]][[fr:Okuni-jinja]][[ja:小国神社]][[zh:小國神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Okuni-jinja (Mori)]][[en:Okuni Shrine]][[fr:Okuni-jinja]][[ja:小国神社]][[zh:小國神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Okuni-jinja (Mori)]][[en:Okuni Shrine]][[fr:Okuni-jinja]][[ja:小国神社]][[zh:小國神社]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = File:Okunijinja-Haiden, Mori-machi Shuchi-gun Shizuoka Japan 2013.06.29.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[:en:Maidono|Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.8475|N|137.89916667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style ={{ill|Taisha-zukuri|WD=Q387874}} | festival = April 14 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Mori|WD=Q1345571}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the town of {{ill|Mori, Shizuoka|en|Mori, Shizuoka|WD=Q1345571|lt=Mori}}, {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|en|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|WD=Q1205501|lt= Shūchi District}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|en|Tōtōmi Province|WD=Q1048864|lt=Tōtōmi Province}}.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei}}) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen}} invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} era of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}}, the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank |国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from {{ill|Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station|en|Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station|WD=Q5370068|lt=Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station}} on the [[:en:Tenryū Hamanako Railroad|Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * {{ill|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|en|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|WD=Q111360896|lt=List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties}} * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Okuni Shrine]] [[en:Oguni shrine]] {{Commons category|Okuni-jinja (Mori)}} [[fr:Okuni-jinja]] [[ja:小国神社]] [[zh:小國神社]] {{wikidata link|Q421597}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:pages]]

小國神社 | image = File:Okunijinja-Haiden, Mori-machi Shuchi-gun Shizuoka Japan 2013.06.29.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[:en:Maidono|Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.8475|N|137.89916667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style ={{ill|Taisha-zukuri|WD=Q387874|1=Taisha-zukuri}} | festival = April 14 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Mori|WD=Q1345571|1=Mori, Shizuoka}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the town of {{ill|Mori, Shizuoka|en|Mori, Shizuoka|WD=Q1345571|lt=Mori|1=Mori, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|en|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|WD=Q1205501|lt= Shūchi District|1=Shūchi District, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|en|Tōtōmi Province|WD=Q1048864|lt=Tōtōmi Province|1=Tōtōmi Province}}.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}}) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen|1=Takeda Shingen}} invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} era of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}, the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank |国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from {{ill|Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station|en|Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station|WD=Q5370068|lt=Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station|1=Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station}} on the [[:en:Tenryū Hamanako Railroad|Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * {{ill|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|en|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|WD=Q111360896|lt=List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|1=List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties}} * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} [[en:Okuni Shrine]] [[en:Oguni shrine]] {{Commons category|Okuni-jinja (Mori)}} [[fr:Okuni-jinja]] [[ja:小国神社]] [[zh:小國神社]] {{wikidata link|Q421597}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Okuni Jinja
小國神社 | image = File:Okunijinja-Haiden, Mori-machi Shuchi-gun Shizuoka Japan 2013.06.29.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' and ''[[:en:Maidono|Maidono]]'' of Okuni Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.8475|N|137.89916667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Okuni Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 3956-1 Mori-machi, Shūchi-gun, Shizuoka-ken 437-0226 | website = {{URL|http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style ={{ill|Taisha-zukuri|WD=Q387874|1=Taisha-zukuri}} | festival = April 14 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Mori|WD=Q1345571|1=Mori, Shizuoka}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Okuni Shrine'''|小國神社|Okuni jinja}}, is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the town of {{ill|Mori, Shizuoka|en|Mori, Shizuoka|WD=Q1345571|lt=Mori|1=Mori, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|en|Shūchi District, Shizuoka|WD=Q1205501|lt= Shūchi District|1=Shūchi District, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, Japan.Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/okuni-jinja/ "Okuni Jinja"]; retrieved 2013-1-27. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former {{ill|Tōtōmi Province|en|Tōtōmi Province|WD=Q1048864|lt=Tōtōmi Province|1=Tōtōmi Province}}.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of Ichinomiya", p. 1]; retrieved 2013-1-27. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Okuni Jinja is: * [[Ōkuninushi|'''Ōnamuchi-no-mikoto''']] (大己貴命), ''kami'' of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== The date of Okuni Shrine's foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition gives a date of February 18, 555 (during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|WD=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}}) as the date when a shrine was first constructed on the summit of six-kilometer distant 511-meter Mount Hongū, which now forms part of the shrine's grounds. The shrine only appears in historical records from an entry within the ''[[Shoku Nihon Kōki]]'' dated June 14, 884 and it is mentioned again in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, but is still listed as a "minor shrine". It has been styled the ''ichinomiya'' of Tōtōmi Province since at least 1235, and continued to be referred to as the “Ichinomiya” until the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. Due to the waning power of the Imperial Court, imperial messages ceased to be sent to the shrine from the Muromachi period. The shrine was destroyed when {{ill|Takeda Shingen|en|Takeda Shingen|WD=Q276404|lt=Takeda Shingen|1=Takeda Shingen}} invaded Tōtōmi in 1572 and the shrine's priests sided with {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}. It was rebuilt by Tokugawa Ieyasu in 1575, and subsequent generations of Tokugawa ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}s'' continued to support the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} era of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}, the shrine was rated as a prefectural shrine in 1873, and was promoted to a {{nihongo|National shrine, 3rd Rank |国幣小社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] in 1874.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 125. The shrine is located a 50-minute walk from {{ill|Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station|en|Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station|WD=Q5370068|lt=Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station|1=Tōtōmi-Ichinomiya Station}} on the [[:en:Tenryū Hamanako Railroad|Tenryū Hamanako Railroad]]{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Events== The main festival of the shrine is held annually on April 18, during which time a set of twelve dances are performed. This performance is said to date to the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} and is a [[Cultural Property (Japan)#Folk Cultural Properties|National Intangible Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190782|title=遠江森町の舞楽 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==Gallery== File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shaden.jpg|Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), torii.jpg|Torii in front of Shaden File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), shinko-sho.jpg|Shinko-sho File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), suginamiki.jpg|approach File:Okuni-jinja (Mori), Kotomachi-ike.jpg| Kotomachi Pond ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * {{ill|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|en|List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|WD=Q111360896|lt=List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties|1=List of Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties}} * [[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] *[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/ Official home page] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Ichinomiya}} {{Commons category|Okuni-jinja (Mori)}} {{wikidata link|Q421597|co|commons:Category:Okuni-jinja (Mori)|en|Okuni Shrine|fr|Okuni-jinja|ja|小国神社|zh|小國神社|en|Oguni shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Mori, Shizuoka]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Tōtōmi Province]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Pages with interwikis with duplicate languages]]
